answer:
**'The Wife's Lament'** (ch. 3 of *Old English Elegies*) depicts a speaker—a wife—exiled and confined to a remote, inhospitable location by her lord amid kin feuds, voicing profound isolation and grief.[^1] A representative passage capturing her physical and emotional confinement:

> I was bid to dwell in a wooded grove,  
> under an oak tree in this earth-cave.  
> Ancient this earth-hall, I am filled with longing.  
> The dales are dim, the dunes are high,  
> bitter the boroughs, with briars overgrown,  
> a home without joy. Often his going  
> has gripped me with grief.[^2]

**Manusmṛti** Book 5 (ch. 5) focuses primarily on dietary laws and purification rather than women's roles directly (though it notes impurities from contact with menstruating women). Domestic constraints on women appear prominently in Book 9 (ch. 9), which prescribes lifelong male guardianship and household duties to prevent ruin. A representative passage:

> Her father protects her in childhood, her husband protects her in youth, and her sons protect her in old age; a woman is never fit for independence.[^3]

> Women must be made dependent by their men day and night; and those who are attached to sense-objects must be kept under one's own control.[^4]

The constraints do not presuppose identical conceptions of womanhood—*The Wife's Lament* (Anglo-Saxon, ~10th c.) portrays a noblewoman enduring punitive isolation amid tribal feuds, emphasizing her loyal suffering and elegiac voice rather than inherent incapacity. Manusmṛti (Indic, ~2nd c. BCE–2nd c. CE) doctrinally frames women as perpetually dependent, sensually fickle, and requiring confinement to household roles for lineage purity and dharma.[^5] Yet both share patriarchal presuppositions: women as vulnerable to external threats (feuds, promiscuity) without male authority, justifying seclusion for protection/order, with deviation risking familial ruin.

[^1]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 3 (/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/03-ik-this-giedd-wreche-bi-me-ful-geomorre/translation/).
[^2]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 3 (/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/03-ik-this-giedd-wreche-bi-me-ful-geomorre/translation/#p-c1cc32).
[^3]: *Manusmṛti*, ch. 9 (/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/09/translation/#p-233021).
[^4]: *Manusmṛti*, ch. 9 (/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/09/translation/#p-27f62f).
[^5]: *Manusmṛti*, ch. 9 (/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/09/translation/#p-026db5).
